questions.question: What is the MVC pattern and how does ASP.NET Core implement it?
questions.answer:
MVC (Model-View-Controller) separates an application into three components:

  • Model — data and business logic. In ASP.NET Core: your domain entities, ViewModels, DbContext. Knows nothing about how data is displayed.
  • View — UI presentation. In ASP.NET Core: Razor .cshtml files that render HTML. Only displays data from the ViewModel.
  • Controller — orchestrator. Receives HTTP requests, calls services/repositories, creates a ViewModel, passes it to the View. No business logic in controllers.

Request lifecycle in ASP.NET Core MVC:

  1. Request arrives → Routing matches URL to controller+action
  2. Action filters run (auth, model validation)
  3. Controller action executes: calls services, builds ViewModel
  4. Returns View(viewModel) → Razor renders HTML
  5. Response sent to browser
public class CategoryController : Controller
{
    private readonly IQuestionService _svc;

    [HttpGet]
    public async Task<IActionResult> Detail(string id)
    {
        var questions = await _svc.GetQuestionsByCategoryAsync(id);
        var vm = new CategoryDetailViewModel { Questions = questions, CategoryId = id };
        return View(vm);  // renders Category/Detail.cshtml
    }
}
followUps: What is a ViewModel and why use it over passing domain models to views?
How does MVC differ from Razor Pages?

followUpAnswers.What is a ViewModel and why use it over:
A ViewModel is a class designed specifically for what a single view needs — it's a projection of your domain model. You should never pass domain models (EF Core entities) directly to views because:

  • Security (over-posting) — If the user POSTs back a domain model, they could set any public property including IsAdmin, UserId, etc. A ViewModel exposes only what the form needs.
  • Flexibility — A view often needs data from multiple domain objects. A ViewModel can flatten or combine them into one class the view can work with cleanly.
  • Display-specific data — ViewModels can carry formatted strings like "₹1,23,456" or "+2.3%", computed flags like IsPositive for CSS classes — things that don't belong in domain entities.
  • Encapsulation — Keeps sensitive fields (password hashes, internal flags) out of the rendered HTML entirely.

Rule of thumb: one ViewModel per view. The ViewModel is the contract between the controller and the view.
followUpAnswers.How does MVC differ from Razor Pages?:
Both are ASP.NET Core rendering models but they organise code differently:

MVCRazor Pages
Controller handles multiple pages/actionsEach page has its own PageModel class
View lives in Views/{Controller}/{Action}.cshtmlView + code live together as Pages/Index.cshtml + Index.cshtml.cs
Good for complex routing, APIs, multi-page featuresGood for simple CRUD pages and form-heavy workflows
More flexibility, more boilerplateLess boilerplate, convention-driven

Razor Pages use OnGet() / OnPost() handlers instead of action methods. For this InterviewPrep project, MVC was chosen because of the complex sidebar navigation, multiple controllers, and shared ViewModels — it fits better than Razor Pages.

questions.tip: Fat Controller anti-pattern: Controllers should be thin orchestrators — receive request, call service, return result. Business logic in controllers is untestable and violates SRP. Move all business logic to services.

questions.question: What is a ViewModel and why should you use it instead of domain models in views?
questions.answer:
A ViewModel is a class designed specifically for what a view needs to display — it's a projection of your domain model tailored to the view's requirements.

Why not pass domain models directly to views?

  • Views often need data from multiple sources — ViewModel can combine Portfolio + Holdings + UserProgress into one flat object
  • Security — domain models may have sensitive fields (password hash, internal flags) you don't want rendered in HTML
  • Over-posting vulnerability — if the user can POST back a domain model, they could set fields you didn't intend (e.g., IsAdmin = true)
  • Views may need display-specific properties — formatted date strings, computed display values
// Domain model (EF Core entity)
public class Portfolio
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string UserId { get; set; } = string.Empty; // don't expose
    public decimal InternalCalculatedValue { get; set; } // sensitive
    // ...
}

// ViewModel — only what the view needs
public class PortfolioSummaryViewModel
{
    public int Id { get; set; }
    public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public string FormattedValue { get; set; } = string.Empty;  // "₹1,23,456"
    public string ChangePercent { get; set; } = string.Empty;   // "+2.3%"
    public bool IsPositive { get; set; }  // for CSS class selection
}
followUps: What is AutoMapper and when should you use it?
What is the Input Model vs ViewModel distinction?

followUpAnswers.What is AutoMapper and when should you u:
AutoMapper is a NuGet library (AutoMapper) that maps properties between objects by convention — matching property names automatically. Instead of writing vm.Name = entity.Name; vm.Email = entity.Email; ... for every property, you configure a Profile and call _mapper.Map<ViewModel>(entity).

// Profile configuration
public class MappingProfile : Profile
{
    public MappingProfile()
    {
        CreateMap<Portfolio, PortfolioSummaryViewModel>()
            .ForMember(dest => dest.FormattedValue,
                       opt => opt.MapFrom(src => src.Value.ToString("C", new CultureInfo("en-IN"))));
    }
}

// Usage in controller
var vm = _mapper.Map<PortfolioSummaryViewModel>(portfolio);

When to use AutoMapper:

  • Large domain objects with 10+ properties that map 1:1 to a ViewModel
  • Consistent mapping needs across many controllers

When NOT to use it:

  • Simple 3–5 property objects — manual mapping is clearer and type-safe
  • Complex transformations — AutoMapper's magic can hide bugs (mismatched names silently map to null)
  • Performance-critical paths — reflection-based mapping adds overhead

Preferred alternative for small projects: Manual mapping in a static extension method or a constructor — explicit, debuggable, no dependency.
followUpAnswers.What is the Input Model vs ViewModel dis:
This is a critical security distinction:

  • ViewModel — used in GET actions. Data flows from server to browser. The controller populates it and passes it to View(vm). It's what the user sees.
  • Input Model (or BindingModel / DTO) — used in POST/PUT actions. Data flows from browser to server. The controller receives it via model binding. It's what the user submits.
// GET — ViewModel (outbound, display)
public IActionResult Create()
{
    var vm = new CreatePortfolioViewModel
    {
        Categories = _svc.GetCategories() // prepopulate dropdown
    };
    return View(vm);
}

// POST — Input Model (inbound, binding)
[HttpPost]
[ValidateAntiForgeryToken]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create(CreatePortfolioInputModel input)
{
    if (!ModelState.IsValid) return View(new CreatePortfolioViewModel { ... });
    await _svc.CreateAsync(input);
    return RedirectToAction(nameof(Index));
}

They can be the same class in simple CRUD but should be separate whenever the domain entity has fields the user shouldn't set (audit fields, computed values, security flags). Separate input models prevent mass assignment / over-posting attacks.

questions.tip: Over-posting attack: If you bind a POST directly to a domain model, a malicious user can set any public property by including it in the form. Use [Bind("Name,Description")] or separate input models (DTOs) for POST/PUT actions.

questions.question: What is TempData and when do you use it?
questions.answer:
TempData stores data that survives exactly one redirect. After the data is read, it's automatically deleted. Used for the Post-Redirect-Get (PRG) pattern — pass a success/error message from a POST action to the GET action it redirects to.

// POST action — after saving, redirect
[HttpPost]
[ValidateAntiForgeryToken]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create(CreatePortfolioViewModel model)
{
    if (!ModelState.IsValid) return View(model);

    await _service.CreateAsync(model);
    TempData["SuccessMessage"] = "Portfolio created successfully!";
    return RedirectToAction(nameof(Index));  // PRG: redirect to GET
}

// GET action — reads and displays the message
public async Task<IActionResult> Index()
{
    ViewBag.Success = TempData["SuccessMessage"];  // TempData auto-cleared after read
    var portfolios = await _service.GetAllAsync();
    return View(portfolios);
}

// In Razor view
@if (ViewBag.Success != null)
{
    <div class="alert alert-success">@ViewBag.Success</div>
}

TempData storage: By default stored in the session. Can use cookies with CookieTempDataProvider.

followUpAnswers.What is TempData.Keep() and Peek()?:
By default, reading a TempData key marks it for deletion after the response. Two methods modify this behaviour:

  • TempData.Keep("key") — reads the value and keeps it alive for one more request. Useful when you read TempData in middleware or an action filter and want the view to also see it.
  • TempData.Peek("key") — reads the value without marking it for deletion. The value remains available for the next read. Use when you need to inspect TempData without consuming it.
// Peek — read without consuming
var msg = TempData.Peek("SuccessMessage") as string;
// TempData["SuccessMessage"] still exists after this read

// Keep — read + preserve for one more hop
var msg = TempData["SuccessMessage"] as string; // marks for deletion
TempData.Keep("SuccessMessage");               // un-marks it

// Keep() with no args preserves ALL TempData keys
TempData.Keep();

In practice these are rarely needed — if you find yourself calling Keep() frequently, it may be a sign that TempData is not the right tool for that flow.
followUpAnswers.TempData vs ViewBag vs ViewData?:
All three pass data from controller to view, but they have very different scopes:

FeatureViewBagViewDataTempData
ScopeCurrent request onlyCurrent request onlySurvives one redirect
Type safetyNone (dynamic)None (dictionary)None (object)
SyntaxViewBag.Title = "x"ViewData["Title"] = "x"TempData["Msg"] = "x"
Use casePass small bits to the view in same requestSame as ViewBag but dictionary syntaxSuccess/error messages after POST→Redirect→GET

ViewBag is just a dynamic wrapper over ViewData — they share the same underlying dictionary. Setting ViewBag.Title is identical to ViewData["Title"].

Preferred approach: Use a strongly-typed ViewModel instead of ViewBag/ViewData for main data. Reserve TempData strictly for PRG flash messages.

questions.tip: PRG pattern is important for browser UX — after a POST, redirecting to GET prevents "Form resubmission" warnings when the user refreshes. Always redirect after a successful POST; use TempData to carry the success message across.

questions.question: What are Tag Helpers in ASP.NET Core Razor views?
questions.answer:
Tag Helpers are server-side components that participate in rendering HTML elements. They replace the older Html Helper syntax with a more HTML-friendly syntax.

<!-- Old Html Helper syntax -->
@Html.ActionLink("Details", "Detail", "Question", new { id = q.Id }, null)
@Html.BeginForm("Create", "Portfolio", FormMethod.Post)

<!-- Tag Helper syntax (cleaner, reads like HTML) -->
<a asp-controller="Question" asp-action="Detail" asp-route-id="@q.Id">Details</a>
<form asp-controller="Portfolio" asp-action="Create" method="post">

<!-- Form elements with model binding -->
<input asp-for="Name" class="form-control" />
<span asp-validation-for="Name" class="text-danger"></span>
<label asp-for="Name"></label>

<!-- Environment-specific content -->
<environment include="Development">
    <script src="~/js/site.js"></script>
</environment>
<environment exclude="Development">
    <script src="~/js/site.min.js" asp-append-version="true"></script>
</environment>

asp-append-version="true" adds a cache-busting hash to the URL — crucial for forcing browsers to reload updated CSS/JS files after deployment.
followUps: How do you create a custom Tag Helper?

followUpAnswers.How do you create a custom Tag Helper?:
Creating a custom Tag Helper requires three steps:

1. Create the class — inherit from TagHelper and override ProcessAsync (or Process for sync):

// Targets <badge level="hard"> elements
[HtmlTargetElement("badge", Attributes = "level")]
public class DifficultyBadgeTagHelper : TagHelper
{
    public string Level { get; set; } = string.Empty;  // maps to 'level' attribute

    public override void Process(TagHelperContext context, TagHelperOutput output)
    {
        output.TagName = "span";  // replace <badge> with <span>
        output.TagMode = TagMode.StartTagAndEndTag;

        var (css, label) = Level.ToLower() switch
        {
            "easy"   => ("badge-easy",   "Easy"),
            "medium" => ("badge-medium", "Medium"),
            "hard"   => ("badge-hard",   "Hard"),
            _        => ("badge-default", Level)
        };

        output.Attributes.SetAttribute("class", $"difficulty-badge {css}");
        output.Content.SetContent(label);
    }
}

2. Register in _ViewImports.cshtml:

@addTagHelper *, InterviewPrepPortal

3. Use in Razor:

<badge level="@q.Difficulty"></badge>
<!-- renders as: <span class="difficulty-badge badge-hard">Hard</span> -->

Tag Helpers can also inject services via constructor DI and use ViewContext for access to the current HTTP context.
followUpAnswers.What is asp-append-version?:
asp-append-version="true" is a built-in Tag Helper attribute for <link>, <script>, and <img> elements. It computes a SHA256 hash of the file's content and appends it as a query string parameter:

<!-- Source -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="~/css/site.css" asp-append-version="true" />

<!-- Rendered HTML -->
<link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/site.css?v=X3n...Zq" />

Why this matters: Browsers and CDNs aggressively cache static files. When you deploy a new version of site.css, the hash changes, so the URL changes, so the browser downloads the new file instead of serving from cache — without you having to manually increment version numbers or invalidate CDN caches.

Important: The hash is computed at startup from the physical file on disk. It only works for static files served from wwwroot. If the file doesn't exist, the attribute is silently ignored (no error).

questions.tip: asp-append-version appends a content hash to the URL: site.css?v=abc123. This busts the CDN/browser cache whenever the file changes, without requiring you to manually update version numbers. Use it on all static asset references.

questions.question: What are partial views and view components? When do you use each?
questions.answer:
Both are reusable view units, but they serve different purposes:

Partial Views — share the rendering context with the parent view. Same ViewData, same model type. Used for breaking large views into smaller files. Synchronous by default (no async support in _PartialName.cshtml).

<!-- Render partial -->
<partial name="_QuestionCard" model="question" />
<!-- Or in controller: return PartialView("_QuestionCard", question) -->

View Components — independent mini-controllers with their own logic and view. Can query the database, inject services, run async code. Used for reusable UI widgets with their own data requirements (sidebar, cart summary, notification bell).

// View Component class
public class CategoryProgressViewComponent : ViewComponent
{
    private readonly IProgressService _svc;
    public CategoryProgressViewComponent(IProgressService svc) => _svc = svc;

    public async Task<IViewComponentResult> InvokeAsync(string categoryId)
    {
        var userId = HttpContext.User.FindFirstValue(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier)!;
        var progress = await _svc.GetCategoryProgressAsync(userId, categoryId);
        return View(progress);
    }
}

// In Razor view
@await Component.InvokeAsync("CategoryProgress", new { categoryId = "di" })
followUps: What is the difference between partial view and layout?
When would you use a View Component vs AJAX?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between partial v:
They serve completely different roles in the view hierarchy:

  • Layout (_Layout.cshtml) — the master shell of the application. It wraps all pages and provides the common structure: navigation bar, sidebar, footer, shared CSS/JS imports, and the @RenderBody() placeholder where each page's content goes. Every page opts into a layout by setting Layout = "_Layout" (or via _ViewStart.cshtml). A page can define named sections (@section Scripts { ... }) that the layout renders with @RenderSection("Scripts", required: false).
  • Partial View (_PartialName.cshtml) — a reusable fragment embedded inside a page's content. It doesn't have a layout of its own. Used to split a large view file into smaller, maintainable pieces that may repeat (e.g., a question card rendered in a loop). Naming convention: prefix with underscore _.
<!-- Layout renders the page shell -->
<!-- _Layout.cshtml -->
<nav>...</nav>
<div class="main">
    @RenderBody()  <!-- each page's content goes here -->
</div>
<footer>...</footer>

<!-- Partial renders a fragment inside a page -->
@foreach (var q in Model.Questions)
{
    <partial name="_QuestionCard" model="q" />
}
followUpAnswers.When would you use a View Component vs A:
Both can update portions of a page but at very different points in the request lifecycle:

ScenarioView ComponentAJAX
TimingInitial page load (server-rendered)After page load (client-initiated)
SEO✅ HTML in initial response❌ Content invisible to crawlers
JavaScript requiredNoYes
Real-time updates❌ No (page must reload)✅ Yes (polling/SignalR)
Partial page update without reload❌ No✅ Yes

Use a View Component when: the widget's data is needed on initial load, you want zero JavaScript, and SEO matters (sidebar navigation, category progress summary).

Use AJAX when: the user triggers an action after page load (saving progress, updating a note), you need real-time updates, or you want to avoid a full page refresh. In this InterviewPrep app, the progress buttons use AJAX fetch() calls to update the DB without reloading the whole page.

questions.tip: Use View Components for server-rendered widgets that need their own data. Use partial views for splitting large views into manageable pieces that share the parent's model. If the widget needs independent async data loading, it must be a View Component — partial views can't run async code independently.
